title: OSPF Neighbors Stuck in INIT Between Core Routers

ticket.summary: Since a rack swap last night, core router core-dc1-r2 is not learning any OSPF routes from core-dc1-r1 over their direct 10G link. Routes that used to be reachable via that link (the 172.20.0.0/16 server subnets) are now missing from core-dc1-r2's routing table, causing intermittent server-to-server connectivity failures in the data center.

topology.description: core-dc1-r1 (10.1.1.1) and core-dc1-r2 (10.1.1.2) are directly connected via a point-to-point 10G link (10.1.1.0/30) and both participate in OSPF area 0. core-dc1-r1 owns the 172.20.0.0/16 server subnets and advertises them into OSPF for core-dc1-r2 to reach. Last night core-dc1-r1 was physically swapped to new hardware and its OSPF configuration was re-applied from a saved template.

logs.content:
Jul 14 05:58:12 core-dc1-r2 %OSPF-5-ADJCHG: Process 1, Nbr 10.1.1.1 on Te0/0/1 from LOADING to FULL
Jul 14 05:59:40 core-dc1-r2 %OSPF-5-ADJCHG: Process 1, Nbr 10.1.1.1 on Te0/0/1 from FULL to DOWN
Jul 14 05:59:41 core-dc1-r2 %OSPF-5-ADJCHG: Process 1, Nbr 10.1.1.1 on Te0/0/1 from DOWN to INIT
Jul 14 06:00:15 core-dc1-r2 %OSPF-5-ADJCHG: Process 1, Nbr 10.1.1.1 on Te0/0/1 from INIT to DOWN
Jul 14 06:00:16 core-dc1-r2 %OSPF-5-ADJCHG: Process 1, Nbr 10.1.1.1 on Te0/0/1 from DOWN to INIT

Jul 14 05:55:02 core-dc1-r1 %SYS-5-CONFIG_I: Configured from memory by console (post-hardware-swap template restore)
Jul 14 05:59:40 core-dc1-r1 %OSPF-5-ADJCHG: Process 1, Nbr 10.1.1.2 on Te0/0/1 from FULL to DOWN, Neighbor Down: Dead timer expired

TenGigabitEthernet0/0/1 is up, line protocol is up
  Internet Address 10.1.1.1/30, Area 0
  Process ID 1, Router ID 10.1.1.1, Network Type POINT_TO_POINT, Cost: 10
  Timer intervals configured, Hello 5, Dead 20, Wait 20, Retransmit 5
  Neighbor Count is 1, Adjacent neighbor count is 0

monitoring.notes: The OSPF adjacency graph in the monitoring dashboard shows core-dc1-r1<->core-dc1-r2 flapping between INIT and DOWN roughly every 40 seconds since 05:59, exactly matching core-dc1-r1's post-swap config reload. The flap period lines up closely with the dead-interval value on core-dc1-r2 (40s), suggesting the two sides disagree on timer values rather than a physical link problem — interface counters show zero CRC errors on both ends.

hints: The physical link is up on both sides and hello packets are clearly being exchanged (neighbor is seen at all) — this points to a parameter mismatch rather than a cabling or Layer 1 issue.
Compare the Hello and Dead timer intervals shown in 'show ip ospf interface' on both routers.
core-dc1-r1 has Hello 5 / Dead 20 while core-dc1-r2 has Hello 10 / Dead 40 — the mismatched hello/dead timers on Te0/0/1 are preventing the adjacency from reaching FULL.

solution.root_cause: When core-dc1-r1's OSPF configuration was restored from a saved template after the hardware swap, it was applied with a different hello-interval (5s) and dead-interval (20s) than core-dc1-r2 (10s/40s). OSPF requires matching hello and dead timers on both ends of a link, so the adjacency repeatedly cycles between INIT and DOWN instead of reaching FULL.
solution.fix: Set matching OSPF hello-interval and dead-interval values on TenGigabitEthernet0/0/1 on both core-dc1-r1 and core-dc1-r2 (e.g. hello 10 / dead 40 on both) so the neighbor relationship can complete and reach FULL.
